cataloging_subject.label: The Stonor Letters and Papers, 1290–1483
cataloging_subject.scope_note: Charles Lethbridge Kingsford's two-volume 1919 editorial reconstruction and selection of Stonor-family documents, represented here by 333 numbered editorial units. The cataloging subject is not one medieval authored work, not the whole historical Stonor archive, not every surviving Stonor document, and not 333 necessarily independent works: individual units may print one or more letters, deeds, accounts, fragments, abstracts, or illustrative records. Kingsford's edition, its archival witnesses, U-M's page-image/OCR encoding, later supplements and reprints, and the repository XML remain distinct lineage layers.

catalog_summary.title: The Stonor Letters and Papers, 1290–1483
catalog_summary.author: Various named and anonymous documentary creators
catalog_summary.date: Documents dated or assigned c. 1290–11 October 1483; edited selection first published 1919
catalog_summary.region: England, especially Stonor and the family's Oxfordshire and Thames Valley networks

agents.name: Stonor family members, correspondents, officials, and other documentary creators
notes: A cataloging collective for the many named and unidentified medieval senders, issuers, accountants, petitioners, clerks, and other creators; it is not a claim of shared authorship or institutional identity.

scope.description: The selected medieval documents, with responsibility varying by item

notes: The schema role author is used broadly for documentary creation; item-level roles include sender, issuer, petitioner, accountant, scribe, and compiler.

scope.description: Selection, reconstruction, ordering, and grouping of dispersed Stonor and illustrative records
Documents printed in full; other selected documents are represented only by abstracts
Document headnotes, editorial dating, introduction, notes, appendices, glossary, and indexes in the print edition

notes: The U-M OCR encodes the numbered documents and their headnotes but omits most introduction and back-matter text.

scope.description: Dates of creation, issue, or editorial assignment for the selected historical documents

notes: Many headings supply approximate or conjectural dates. The lower endpoint belongs to Kingsford's title and his dating of item 1; the current TNA catalog gives that deed the broader range 1200–1300.

scope.description: First publication of Kingsford's two-volume edited selection
Principal political, legal, social, and economic setting of the selected documents

notes: Individual documents also concern or were written in continental locations, including Valladolid, Calais, and Bruges; England is not asserted as the place of every item's creation.

scope.description: Core county of the Stonor family's estates and public activity
Ancestral home and organizing family association

language_statements.label: English

scope.description: Kingsford's titles, abstracts, headnotes, introduction, notes, and reference matter

notes: The U-M header declares only eng; direct inspection shows that this is not a complete description of the historical documents' languages.

form_statements.description: A prose documentary collection comprising letters, charters, legal and administrative records, accounts, inventories, petitions, memoranda, and editorial abstracts.

genre_statements.label: Correspondence

scope.description: The large letter component, especially the fifteenth-century family and business correspondence

notes: Kingsford counted 256 letters excluding fragments; neither that count nor the title makes every numbered unit a letter.

scope.description: Deeds, charters, petitions, writs, court and shrieval records, and estate documentation
Household, estate, rental, expense, and other accounts

subject_statements.label: Wars of the Roses and political news

summaries.value: Kingsford's edited collection reconstructs a dispersed archive associated with the Stonor family, established Oxfordshire gentry whose legal, estate, household, mercantile, familial, and political networks extended across England and beyond. The selected records range from a thirteenth-century charter to Francis Lovell's letter of 11 October 1483 and include family and business correspondence, deeds, petitions, writs, accounts, rentals, inventories, and other records. The collection is especially rich for fifteenth-century gentry life, literacy and letter writing, estate and household management, legal practice, commerce and the wool trade, and political experience during the Wars of the Roses.

The 1919 edition has 333 numbered units: volume I contains items 1–158 and volume II contains items 159–333. Kingsford printed almost all identified letters except fragments he judged too slight, supplied full texts or abstracts of selected deeds, included selected Chancery Miscellanea material, and added a few illustrative records that he expressly said did not belong to the historical archive. His print volumes also contain an introduction, appendices and calendars, a glossary, and indexes; the U-M electronic text omits most of that front and back matter while retaining linked page milestones.

This record describes Kingsford's 1919 editorially created selection as the browsable corpus work. Component-document dates, languages, genres, authorship, and archive references are scoped to selected passages rather than propagated to every unit. Later recovered material, the 1924 supplement, Carpenter's 1996 reissue, modern scans, U-M OCR/markup, and current TNA catalog descriptions are documented in lineage rather than conflated with the selected work.

extent.description: The repository XML contains 333 body DIV1 item units, numbered 1–333, and 55 nested DIV2 sections. These are edition/encoding units, not a count of distinct archival objects: some units combine multiple documents, fragments, abstracts, or editorial material. Volume I contains 158 numbered units and volume II contains 175.

content_parts.label: Volume I: items 1–158
content_parts.summary: The first 158 numbered documentary units, from the charter of Richard de Stonor assigned c. 1290 through material dated or assigned to 1475.

content_parts.label: Volume II: items 159–333
content_parts.summary: The final 175 numbered documentary units, continuing the collection through Francis Lovell's letter to Sir William Stonor of 11 October 1483.

related_works.title: Supplementary Stonor Letters and Papers (1314–1482)
notes: Kingsford's 1924 supplement is related scholarship, not an upstream source of the 1919 edition or U-M encoding.

related_works.title: Kingsford's Stonor Letters and Papers 1290–1483
notes: Carpenter's 1996 publication reissues the 1919 volumes and 1924 supplement with new introduction, annotation, and a supplementary listing; it is not upstream of CME.

evidence.summary: Supplies the encoded title, U-M identifier, 1919 source edition, document headings and contents, languages visible in the text, subject headings, OCR statement, page-image references, and omitted-matter structure.

evidence.citation: Element and source-citation inspection of repository aca1723.xml.
evidence.locator: FRONT/TITLEPAGE; BODY/DIV1 and DIV2; PB, P, NOTE1, SIGNED, SALUTE, TABLE, and FIGURE elements
evidence.summary: Counts 333 numbered BODY DIV1 item units, 55 DIV2 sections, 798 paragraphs, 460 page milestones, 123 notes, 207 signatures, 205 salutations, no verse lines or groups, two title pages, 67 front page milestones, 332 body page milestones, and 61 back page milestones; confirms the item split 1–158 and 159–333.

evidence.citation: Charles Lethbridge Kingsford, ed., The Stonor Letters and Papers, 1290–1483, 2 vols., Camden Third Series 29–30 (1919).
evidence.summary: Title pages and complete scans confirm title, editor, publication, two-volume structure, document numbering, contents, languages, and print front/back matter.

evidence.citation: Kingsford, introduction to The Stonor Letters and Papers, vol. I, pp. xxxvi–xlvii.
evidence.summary: Explains the reconstructed archive, documentary selection, full-text and abstract policy, added illustrative records, source citations, approximate chronology, spelling policy, and the count of 256 letters and more than 600 historical documents.

evidence.citation: The National Archives, current Discovery records for Stonor source materials.
evidence.summary: Supports current archive-class mappings and the distinction between Kingsford's historical designations, current shelfmarks, and current catalog dates.

evidence.citation: Elizabeth Noble, The World of the Stonors: A Gentry Society (2009), publisher preview.
evidence.summary: Supports the collection's social-historical scope, the mix of letters and non-letter records, Kingsford's approximate archive count, later recovered material, and the distinction between later witnesses and the 1919 source chain.

evidence.citation: Anglo-Norman Dictionary, bibliography entry for The Stonor Letters and Papers.
evidence.summary: Identifies a group of twenty-four Anglo-Norman Stonor letters dated 1325–1380 and provides page references in the Carpenter reissue.

evidence.citation: Royal Historical Society, Camden Third Series list.
evidence.summary: Confirms the 1919 edition as Camden Third Series volumes 29–30 and the 1924 supplement as volume 34.

evidence.citation: aca1723 lineage manifest boundary and relation audit.
evidence.summary: Supports keeping later-recovered C 47/37/22 and C 47/37/23 material outside the 1919/CME upstream chain.

evidence.citation: Kingsford, Supplementary Stonor Letters and Papers (1314–1482), 1924.
evidence.summary: Identifies the later supplement and its publication facts.

evidence.citation: Christine Carpenter, Kingsford's Stonor Letters and Papers 1290–1483 (1996).
evidence.summary: Identifies the later reissue, its inclusion of the 1919 volumes and 1924 supplement, and its new scholarly matter.

open_questions.question: Can all 333 numbered editorial units be cataloged at component level without treating grouped documents, abstracts, and fragments as equivalent units?
next_steps: Create an item-level crosswalk recording each heading, documentary type, creator, recipient, language, date status, archival source, and whether Kingsford printed a full text or abstract.
Preserve multiple source documents within one numbered unit rather than forcing a one-to-one model.

open_questions.question: Should the collection's lower date remain Kingsford's c. 1290 or be displayed with the current TNA catalog's broader 1200–1300 range for item 1?
next_steps: Review the deed C 146/221 diplomatically and consult current scholarship on Richard de Stonor and Cicely.
Continue to attribute c. 1290 to Kingsford rather than silently replacing or hardening it.

open_questions.question: What are the exact document-level language counts, including mixed-language and editorially abstracted units?
next_steps: Perform item-level language tagging against the scans and source records.
Do not use the U-M header's eng-only declaration as a document-level language inventory.

notes: The header value DA 20 .R9 Ser.3 v.29-30 is a bibliographic/library call number for the Camden volumes, not a manuscript or archival shelfmark.
The 333 numbered units are Kingsford's editorial sequence; the historical archive was larger, dispersed, and subsequently augmented by recovered material.
Document dates and titles often encode Kingsford's editorial judgment and should not be substituted for current repository descriptions without attribution.
